title: Support Kubernetes style rolling updates
body:
### Feature request description

I see on the [documentation](https://docs.podman.io/en/latest/markdown/podman-kube-play.1.html#deployment-fields) that the deployment `strategy.x` fields are not currently supported in podman-kubernetes yaml, which from what I understand are how you'd normally set up rolling updates in a kubernetes system. Podman already has auto-update support which is really awesome, and I think adding rolling updates functionality on top of that would really make podman shine above docker in this regard, and it would take away one of the advantages that a full kubernetes orchestrator has over podman currently. 

### Suggest potential solution

I'm sure I'm naive but it would seem "simple" to implement some of this functionality on top of the auto-update functionality (to first order, just start the new container before stopping old container), so I'm optimistic that this is possible :) 

### Have you considered any alternatives?

The only alternatives I'm aware of would be user-spun scripting solutions or migrating to full kubernetes ._.

### Additional context

I'd rather not have to spin up a full kubernetes stack to get this feature.  Personally I don't particularly care if this was actually implemented via the kubernetes yaml or some other method.

comments.body:
This doesn't sound like something podman should do and I am not even sure how this could even work on a single node.
When you use a published port or writeable volume (i.e. db) it would be impossible to start the new one while the old one is still running.

Besides that this would be anything but simple with the current auto-update structure. auto-update works by pulling new images and then simply restarts the systemd unit. If you require a new service it would require new systemd service then as the old one is still running. That is not something we can do as we don't directly manage the units as they are user managed.

So overall I would say this is not something we should support.

Hmm, thanks for humoring my request. I see your point about the systemd and port conflicts making this less trivial than I was hoping.  

I still feel I have a use case here which feels fairly well defined, and I'm trying to sort out what good options I have. I would like something close to rolling-updates on a single node, or minimizing downtime by spinning up multiple versions of a container at once, performing appropriate health checks and handoffs before bringing down the old container. The containers themselves clearly need to support this with appropriate port and volume handling and health checks and the like.

I guess another approach would be for a single podman systemd service to be able to manage multiple pods (Which would also seem to enable "replicas" more properly), but I assume this is far from the way podman works at the moment.

I guess my main other options are mostly in the spirit of manually defining multiple services and writing my own handling for the smooth handover between them, maybe trying to use systemd to facilitate parts of that.

> I guess my main other options are mostly in the spirit of manually defining multiple services and writing my own handling for the smooth handover between them, maybe trying to use systemd to facilitate parts of that.

I believe that's the way to go.  As @Luap99 mentioned above: there is no general one-size-fits-all solution on a single node. So writing use-case specific logic around it can make sense.  Maybe Quadlet/systemd templating can help spin up multiple flavors of the same service?

Reading the initial request, I also thought about the same issues that @Luap99 raised. BTW there are the same issues that made us disregard the `replicas` field of a `Deployment` and always use `1`.
As a result, while there could be a solution that works with a list of caveats, I think it will prove as both a nightmare to maintain and not-that-usable for users.
So, while I see the value in this request, I just don't see it happening
